intent: a personal dashboard for tracking long-term health experiments

body.intent: a personal dashboard for tracking long-term health experiments

draft.title: a personal dashboard for tracking long-term health experiments
draft.summary: Drafting your prototype from the swipes…
draft.html:
a personal dashboard for tracking long-term health experiments

Building your first draft…

data.intent: a personal dashboard for tracking long-term health experiments

facade.hypothesis: For health experiment tracking, a persistent left sidebar navigation combined with tab-based content switching will help users quickly jump between experiments while keeping context visible
facade.axisTargeted: Navigation architecture (sidebar vs tabs) and information density
facade.label: Dense sidebar with tabs
facade.acceptImplies: User prefers consolidated navigation—sidebar + tabs creates efficient experiment switching without losing overview of all tracked items
facade.rejectImplies: User wants simpler, flatter navigation—either full-page tabs or card-based discovery without persistent sidebar complexity

facade.hypothesis: Users prefer a friendly, human-centered voice that feels like talking to a knowledgeable friend rather than reading a clinical report
facade.axisTargeted: Voice and Personality — friendly vs professional
facade.label: Warm and conversational
facade.acceptImplies: Adopt warm, encouraging language; use 'you' and 'we'; celebrate small wins; make data feel approachable and motivating
facade.rejectImplies: Move toward clinical precision, neutral tone, minimal personality; prioritize accuracy and objectivity over warmth; use passive constructions and technical language

evidence.hypothesis: For health experiment tracking, a persistent left sidebar navigation combined with tab-based content switching will help users quickly jump between experiments while keeping context visible
evidence.implication: User prefers consolidated navigation—sidebar + tabs creates efficient experiment switching without losing overview of all tracked items

facade.hypothesis: Users tracking health data prefer high contrast and clear visual hierarchy to quickly scan metrics
facade.label: Dark cards, light background
facade.acceptImplies: Explore darker card-based UI with bright accent colors for data readout; test dark mode variants
facade.rejectImplies: Move toward lighter, more minimal aesthetic; test soft neutral palettes with subtle shadows instead

facade.hypothesis: A health dashboard can feel encouraging and human through color (warm amber/terracotta tones) and rounded edges, rather than cool clinical blues and sharp grids.
facade.label: Warm data viz
facade.acceptImplies: User wants the dashboard to feel emotionally supportive and approachable—prioritize color warmth, soft typography, and data presentation that feels like a friendly conversation.
facade.rejectImplies: User prefers a clinical, neutral, or cool aesthetic—prioritize cool tones (grays, blues), minimal color, and a more detached professional feel.

facade.hypothesis: Users prefer animated state transitions over instant data refreshes when viewing health metrics
facade.axisTargeted: Fluid animated transitions vs instant static updates
facade.label: Smooth slide-in updates
facade.acceptImplies: Build with spring animations for metric changes, data entry confirmations, and chart redraws
facade.rejectImplies: Prioritize instant feedback and minimal motion; use snappy micro-interactions or static reveals instead

facade.hypothesis: Users want continuous trend visualization (one metric per sparkline) over dense data tables for tracking health experiment progress
facade.axisTargeted: Charts vs Text — do they prefer visual trends or tabular precision?
facade.label: Line charts over time
facade.acceptImplies: Build with interactive time-series charts as primary view; tables are secondary/export
facade.rejectImplies: Lean into tabular data with sortable columns and raw numbers; charts may feel like decoration

axes.label: Whether user prefers minimal clinical aesthetics vs. warm, human-centered visual design for health data
Whether user wants data-dense dashboard layout vs. progressive disclosure with one metric at a time
axes.poleA: All at once or step by step?

axes.label: Whether user responds to motivational/encouraging tone vs. neutral, factual reporting voice
Whether user wants celebratory, gamified feedback vs. calm, meditative tracking environment
Whether user prefers raw numbers and charts vs. narrative summaries and insights
Whether user wants instant feedback on actions vs. slow, contemplative animations and transitions

facade.hypothesis: For health experiment tracking, displaying experiments as individual cards in a grid layout will make each experiment feel like a discrete entity that's easier to scan and compare, rather than a dense linear list that requires more cognitive load
facade.axisTargeted: Information density and scanning behavior — cards (spacious, discrete) vs lists (dense, linear)
facade.label: Cards over list view
facade.acceptImplies: User gravitates toward modular, glanceable layouts that prioritize visual separation and quick experiment comparison over compact information density
facade.rejectImplies: User prefers compact, streamlined presentation — likely favors lists or tables that minimize scrolling and show more data per screen without visual fragmentation
